result:
# The Smell of Old Books and What It Means to Lose a Sense

I want to talk about petrichor first, because petrichor is one of those words that people discover and immediately want to use in a sentence, any sentence, just to have said it. *The smell of rain on dry earth.* There's a compound responsible for it — geosmin, produced by soil bacteria called actinomycetes — and knowing this chemical fact somehow makes the smell both more and less interesting at the same time. More interesting because the earth is literally exhaling microbial breath at you when rain falls. Less interesting because naming a thing is the first step toward making it ordinary.

But petrichor is what I'm thinking about as I try to work out what I actually want to write about, which is olfaction — the sense of smell — and specifically the strange cultural position it occupies. Smell is the most emotionally loaded of the senses, tied more directly to memory than any other, routing through the limbic system in ways that vision and hearing simply don't. And yet it's also the sense we talk about the least, the one we have the fewest words for, the one that embarrasses us sometimes, the one that COVID-19 stole from millions of people and in doing so made suddenly, desperately precious.

Let me start with the books.

---

There's a word — *bibliosmia* — which refers to the smell of old books, though the word itself is barely in any dictionary and probably functions more as a pleasing neologism than established vocabulary. But the thing it describes is real and widely beloved. You walk into a second-hand bookshop or an old library and there's something waiting for you — vanilla and almonds and something faintly woody, something that smells like time itself if time had a texture. Chemists have actually studied this. The smell is produced by the slow degradation of paper: lignin breaking down into vanillin, which is chemically related to vanilla. Older paper releases benzaldehyde, which smells of almonds. There are hundreds of volatile organic compounds involved. The smell of old books is the smell of decay, technically. It is the slow chemical process of dissolution making itself known to you in the most romantic possible way.

This seems significant to me. The thing we find comforting and nostalgic and associated with knowledge and warmth is fundamentally a process of disintegration. The book is telling you it's dying. And we breathe it in and feel peaceful.

There's something in that dynamic — smell revealing truths that other senses dress up or conceal — that keeps drawing me back. Vision, after all, is extraordinarily easy to manipulate. We understand this. Cinema is built on the manipulation of vision. Advertising rewires how things look. We spend extraordinary effort making things appear different from what they are. Hearing is somewhat harder to deceive but still remarkably tractable — we pipe music into spaces specifically to alter how people feel, how long they stay, how much they spend. Smell is trickier. We can add smells — casinos reportedly pump oxygen and certain fragrances to keep people alert and betting; supermarkets pipe bread smells from the bakery throughout the store — but there's a rawness to smell, an inability to completely fake it, that means it keeps delivering true information. A building that smells of mold is moldy, no matter how fresh the paint looks. A person who smells of fear — and this is a real thing, not just a metaphor; people do emit different compounds when frightened — is frightened, even if their face is composed. The body broadcasts on frequencies that vision can't jam.

---

Helen Keller wrote about smell as "the fallen angel of the senses." She had reason to think carefully about senses that most people take for granted, and her conclusion was that smell was the most underappreciated, the one that could have given the most and was instead mostly ignored. She described smelling a passing stranger and being able to construct a whole person from that information — their profession, their recent activities, something of their character. Whether this was literallyaccurate or somewhat lyrical, I don't know. But it points toward something real: smell is informative in ways we've trained ourselves to stop registering.

Animals haven't made this error. A dog's olfactory epithelium has about 300 million receptor cells compared to a human's 6 million. A dog can detect certain cancers, track a person across terrain days later, identify a family member in a crowd from a scent alone. We tend to position this as a remarkable dog fact rather than a human deficiency, but maybe that framing is wrong. Maybe the more useful observation is that humans, somewhere in the course of becoming the cognitive creatures we became, partially abandoned one of their richest information channels. We built language instead, which is remarkable and transformative and allows me to write this sentence, but also substitutes a described world for a directly experienced one in ways that are not always improvements.

The anthropologist Constance Classen has argued that the history of smell in Western culture is largely a history of increasing suppression. Older cultures — and many contemporary non-Western cultures — organized social life much more explicitly around smell. Certain smells were sacred, certain smells were profane. Aromatics were political. Frankincense and myrrh were valuable partly because burning them created a specific olfactory environment that signaled the presence of the sacred, that altered the mood and physiology of everyone in the space. The Western Enlightenment, with its emphasis on vision as the rational sense — on observation, on the visible, on the eye of reason — pushed smell toward the background. Smell became associated with the irrational, the animal, the bodily, the embarrassing. We perfumed ourselves to mask natural smells and then felt vaguely ashamed of the perfume too.

Meanwhile, the vocabulary failed to develop. English has almost no primary smell words — words that specifically denote a smell without reference to its source or to another sense. *Pungent* means penetrating, which is a tactile metaphor. *Acrid* refers to sharpness, another tactile metaphor. *Fragrant* just means pleasant-smelling without specifying what kind of pleasant. Compare this to the color vocabulary, or the vocabulary for sounds, or taste. We have *sweet, sour, bitter, salty, umami* as primary taste categories with wide cultural elaboration. We have hue and saturation and brightness and an endless proliferation of named colors. We have pitch and timbre and rhythm for sound. For smell, we mostly say "it smells like..." and then name the source: it smells like roses, it smells like rain, it smells like something burning. We are pointing rather than describing. We are using our smell vocabulary to defer to vision — the named object — rather than to characterize the sensation directly.

Some languages do better. The Jahai people of the Malay Peninsula have been studied by linguist Asifa Majid, who found they possess a rich vocabulary for smells that allows them to describe olfactory qualities as abstractly and precisely as English speakers describe colors. They are not pointing at sources; they are naming properties. This suggests the poverty is cultural and linguistic, not inherent. We could have built the vocabulary. We chose differently.

---

Which brings me to what happened when COVID-19 removed the sense of smell from millions of people.

Anosmia — the loss of smell — had always existed, caused by injury, illness, aging, various other factors. There is a small and genuinely devoted community of people who live with it permanently, who have been trying to explain to the olfactory-equipped majority what it's like. And what they describe is not simply the absence of smell. It is the absence of an entire emotional register. Food becomes fuel. Memory loses a trigger. Arriving home loses something. The world becomes slightly flattened in a way that's difficult to convey to people who haven't experienced it, because you can't demonstrate the absence of a sense to someone who still has it.

When COVID took smell from people who had previously not thought much about it, the accounts were striking. Person after person describing not just the missing smells but the missing *attachment to the world*. The smell of a newborn child. The smell of a partner's skin. The smell of coffee, not as a treat but as a daily recalibration, a sensory event that says: this is the morning, this is the beginning, you are here. When these were removed, people reported not just inconvenience but something closer to grief. A flattening of affect. A sense of being behind glass.

The philosopher of mind might note here that this suggests smell is doing more emotional and cognitive work than we typically give it credit for. We think of attention as a resource we deploy consciously, but many of our attention-orienting mechanisms are sensory and automatic. Smell is constantly reading the environment and generating low-level emotional responses — comfort, alertness, unease, appetite — that we experience as moods or dispositions without knowing their source. Remove smell and the mood regulation suffers. You don't know why you feel slightly less grounded. The signal is gone but you don't have language for the signal so you can't name the absence.

There's also the memory dimension, which is probably the most discussed aspect of olfaction even though it remains somewhat mysterious mechanically. The connection between smell and memory is not subtle. Proust built the most famous passage in French literature around it — the madeleine dipped in tea, releasing a smell that unlocks an entire childhood — and while it's become a slightly tired reference, the phenomenon itself is genuine and replicable and strange. Smell memories are often older and more emotionally charged than memories encoded through other senses. They bypass the same filtering that other sensory memories pass through. They arrive with an urgency, a presentness, that can be disorienting: you are suddenly *there*, in that kitchen, in that school hallway, at that moment, with a vividness that visual memory usually doesn't provide.

Why? The anatomy offers a partial answer. The olfactory nerve connects directly to the amygdala and hippocampus — the brain regions most centrally involved in emotion and memory — without the relay through the thalamus that other senses require. Smell has a more direct line to the emotional core of the brain. It was there before the cerebral cortex elaborated into its current complexity. It is an old system talking to other old systems, without the neocortical intermediary that processes and moderates and rationalizes. When a smell triggers a memory, you're not retrieving and reconstructing in the way that recalling a visual scene involves; you're being pulled somewhere, as if the smell were still happening, as if you were still there.

This is why anosmia, in people who develop it after a lifetime of smelling, produces what some researchers have called a kind of autobiographical disruption. The smell-memory connection isn't just about pleasant nostalgic moments. It's about the continuous sense of having been a continuous person — of the present being connected to the past through something immediate and sensory. Take that away and the timeline gets thinner.

---

I keep returning to something that feels like a larger point about the things we don't have words for.

The weakness of smell vocabulary is an example of a more general truth: that we navigate by what we can name, and anything that falls outside the naming system becomes difficult to think about and easy to ignore. This isn't a novel observation — Wittgenstein gestured at it, Orwell worried about it in a political register, contemporary cognitive scientists study it empirically — but it seems to acquire a particular vividness when you're thinking about smell, because smell is such a clear example of a channel of genuine richness that we've let go partly fallow.

What else have we let go fallow? What other information streams are we receiving but not processing, not discussing, not developing vocabularies for? Interoception — the sense of the internal state of the body, the vague sense of one's own physiology — is probably another. We're not great at it. We're famously poor at knowing when we're hungry versus when we're thirsty, when we're tired versus when we're sad, when our blood sugar is low versus when we're anxious. The body is talking continuously and we attend intermittently, when the signal gets loud enough to break through.

There's probably a version of this essay to be written about every sense that gets undervalued, every information channel we have that we've failed to fully develop. But smell feels like the most poignant case because it sits at this odd intersection: most directly tied to memory and emotion, most lacking in vocabulary, most connected to things we care about enormously — connection to places, connection to people, the texture of daily life — and somehow least discussed, least celebrated, most taken for granted right up until it's gone.

When it's gone, people understand what it was. This is the common human structure: understanding a thing through its absence. Grief teaches you what love was doing. Illness teaches you what health was doing. Anosmia, it turns out, teaches you that smell was doing the work of continuous orientation — that it was quietly affirming, thousands of times a day, that you were somewhere specific, that specific things were happening, that you were present in the world.

The old books smell of their own dying. The rain smells of living soil. The morning coffee says *here, now, you*. And none of it requires a word from you. It is just there, operating, doing its patient work, connecting you to everything that has ever smelled like this to you, sending its chemical messages along the oldest pathways in your brain, asking nothing except that you breathe.

Which is, it turns out, not nothing.

---

I don't have a tidy conclusion. The sense of smell doesn't resolve into a lesson. What I'm left with is something more like an instruction: pay attention to it, the way you'd pay attention to something you knew you might lose. Smell the old books. Smell the rain. Smell the people you love, which sounds strange until you've lost it and then sounds like the most basic thing in the world.

Develop whatever vocabulary you can. *This smells like Saturday morning. This smells like every good summer. This smells like safety.* Even if the words are clumsy and referring to sources rather than qualities, even if you're pointing rather than describing, it's better than silence. The naming is a way of registering. The registering is a way of keeping.

The fallen angel of the senses. The one that takes the most direct path to where you actually live.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
